content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration · · · Executive/Legal · · · Enforcement/Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660                                322-5901                           322-6441

March 29, 1977

Mr. Anthony J. Piazza
City Attorney
City of Victorville
14343 Civic Drive
Victorville, California 92392

Dear Mr. Piazza:

This is in response to your letter of January 25 and previous tele-
phone calls concerning the possibility of the existence of a conflict of
interest on the part of certain city officials in the matter of a rezoning
of certain pieces of property. As we discussed on the telephone, the
staff agrees generally with the arguments and conclusions made in your
letter. Since the question posed does not present a substantial question
of interpretation of the Political Reform Act, the Commission will not issue
a formal opinion but will, pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329(b),
offer the following informal advice.

Based on the information on pages two and three of your letter, and
the fact that the property in which the Council Person and Planning Director
have an interest is already developed to maximum density, we agree with
your conclusion that it is not "foreseeable that the decision will have
a material financial effect" on that interest in real property. Government
Code Sections 87100 and 87103. See also our regulations on "Material
Financial Effect", 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702.

This advice is of course limited to the specific rezoning question
cited in your letter. If 12 can be of any further assistance please feel
free to call me at (916) 322-6444. Thank you for writing and please
forgive my delay in responding.

Sincerely,

Kenneth W. Goshorn
Research Specialist III
Conflicts of Interest Division

KWC:mfa

CITY OF

VICTORVILLE

714-245-3411
14343 Civic Drive
Victorville, California 92392

January 25, 1977


Mr. John Klepinger
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Mr. Klepinger:

A matter has recently come before the City Council in which I was
asked whether or not a member of the City Council and Director of
Planning should disqualify themselves from discussion on a matter.
I informed them they need not disqualify themselves, and since the
question has been raised as to whether or not I am correct, I am
requesting your guidance in this matter. I will attempt to chrono-
logically set forth the history of the matter and request your kind
assistance.

I.

A. There are four pieces of property on La Paz Drive between Pebble
Beach Drive and Hughes Road in the City of Victorville (see attached
Addendum "A"). Ordinance No. 7, which was adopted on December 21,
1964, pursuant to Zone Change No. 20, changed the first three lots
from R-1, Single Family Residential, to R-3, Multiple Residential
lots. On April 25, 1969, pursuant to Zone Change No. 68, Ordinance
No. 200, the fourth lot (marked number 4) was changed from R-1 to
R-3. This was the outgrowth of a General Plan study which was
conducted in 1965 and 1966.

B. Pursuant to the Victorville Municipal Code, Section 18.20.010,
which is the intent section of the City's old R-3 zoning code,
between 9 and 30 units could be built on R-3 property.

C. In 1974 and 1975, an independent agency was asked to come in
and revise the City's General Plan. After two years of study, this
was accomplished and in the latter part of 1975 that plan was presented
to the City Council for study, and was subsequently adopted during
the January meeting in 1976.

Mr. John Klepinger                    - 2 -                    January 25, 1977


D.    Pursuant to that study, the four lots in question were rezoned to
Very High Density Multiple Residential lots, or R-4. However, under
the new code sections (Section 18.20.020), R-4 only permits a maximum
of 20 dwelling units per acre, which is 10 dwelling units less than the
maximum possible under the old R-3 section.

E.    Title VII of Planning and Zoning Law mandates that the zone must
conform to the General Plan (see Section 65860 of the Government Code).
I specifically call your attention to Subsection (c) which states that
conformance within the General Plan must take place as soon as reasonably
possible.

                                    II.

A.    Fairway Manor Apartment House was built in 1972 on lot 1; also, in
1972 La Paz Townhouses was built on what has been previously been described
as Lot Number 4. Lots Number 2 and 3 are both unimproved and the ownership
of Lots 1, 2 and 3 rest with persons other than those involved in the
question before us.

B.    The La Paz Townhouses were purchased in February and March, 1976,
after the adoption of the General Plan by a limited partnership known
as La Paz Townhouses, Ltd. The City Attorney is a general partner and
a member of the Council is a limited partner of that association.

C.    An application was made some time later in 1976 to the Real Estate
Commissioner requesting that the apartments be converted to condominiums,
and this request was granted, and the figure set on the value for sale of
each condominium was $19,950.

D.    Subsequent to that time, the City Director of Planning, in approximately
November, 1976, purchased one of the condominiums and the City Attorney
purchased four units from the aforementioned limited partnership.

                                    III.

A.    January 17, 1977, Ordinance No. 501 was presented to the City Council
for adoption to bring zoning into conformance with the General Plan as
mandated. This was a extensive ordinance covering multiple properties
identified as properties "A" through "I". That property designated as
"P" included Lots 1, 2, 3 and 4 previously alluded to.

B.    At that time the Council person involved asked the City Attorney whether
or not she should disqualify herself, and the City Attorney advised that it was
not necessary to do so. She then participated in the discussion only to the
following extent:

     "On La Paz there are already these two large developments. Have
we had any problems you are aware of with traffic on the streets
presently being logged, or is everything running along smoothly?
Have you had any comment, protest either way."

Mr. John Klepinger                    - 3 -                    January 25, 1977

C. The City Attorney did not participate in the discussion at all.

D. That property in this was neither approved or disapproved that evening
but the matter was referred back to the Planning Commission along with
some other parcels for further study and review. The motion to refer the
matter back was not made by the Council person in question.

E. The City Council adjourned the regular meeting until the next evening
to finish the agenda, and dialogue took place which is referenced as
Addendum "B".

                                        IV.

I did not feel it was necessary for the Council person or Planning Director
to disqualify themselves for the following reasons:

A. Voting to bring zoning into conformance with adopted General Plan is
really an act that is administerial in nature and in fact, mandated by State
statute.

B. Although, under Section 87100 all three parties had an interest in the
property that exceeded $1,000, it does not appear that even if their action
was discretionary it would be "reasonably forseeable that the decision will
have no material effect distinguishable from its affect on the public
generally", in that under the old zone they could have a maximum of 30 units
per acre where under the new zone, they would be limited to 20 units per
acre.

C. Since the apartments were already built, and in fact, preceded both the
new General Plan and the conforming zone request, the value of their property
would not be affected, and in essence, only those parcels, Lots 2 and 3,
could possibly be affected since they were not developed and none of the
parties involved had ownership interest.

D. Since the Council person was only a limited partner, she has, by law, no
direct management control of the property marked number 4.

I realize that I may have made a mistake, if not legally, at least politically,
in that the Council person and City Attorney and Director of Planning should
have stated all of the above on the record. However, I do believe I am
correct in my interpretation of the law, and although it appears to be a
moot question since no action was taken, nor were any statements influencing
the action made, I would request guidance from you for future matters of this
nature.

Your earliest attention and guidance in this matter will be greatly appreciated.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Anthony J. Piazza
                                        City Attorney

AJP:jar
Attachments

ADDENDUM "A"

HUGHES RD

④

LAPAZ CONDOMINIUMS

③

VACANT LAND

②

VACANT LAND

①

PEBBLE BEACH APTS.

PEBBLE BEACH DR

LAPAZ K

4
N

ADDENDUM "B"

VERBATIM TRANSCRIPT OF PORTION OF
VICTORVILLE CITY COUNCIL MEETING
JANUARY 18, 1977


Mrs. Sartor - Before we go to the Executive Session, I have a personal
statement I want to make. This morning Mark Dever called me relative
to why I participated or why I didn't disqualify myself relative to one
of the items on last night's agenda. I would like to make sure there is
no doubt in anyone's mind. I did go over and ask Mr. Piazza when this
item came up on what I should do and he told me what to do and I acted on
it during the break. We discussed again this matter during the break. I
would like to state that if there is any doubt on the legality of my
participation, I will disqualify myself in the future because my husband
and I do own a very small part of a company, corporation, or whatever -
we are limited partners in an organization that does have a financial
interest in property on La Paz. We don't have anything to do with the
property, we just have a share of the corporation.

Mr. Caldwell - I don't think you should be disqualified. If the opinion
is that you should, then I should disqualify myself from the La Paz Drive
issue because I think I probably live within the parameter or distance
inasmuch as I live at the corner of Burning Tree and Lakewood. I never
thought about it.

Mrs. Sartor - I did ask Mr. Piazza.

City Attorney - First of all, Ms. Sartor is correct. I was sitting here
when the session was going on and Mrs. Sartor walked over and asked if
she had to disqualify herself because she had an interest in a limited
partnership as a limited partner. I told her she did not have to disqualify
herself because by the very nature of the partnership she had no control or
management, only the general partners do. Number two, it is a moot question
because no action was taken. Mrs. Sartor talked with me about it again at
the break and I told her again that I felt she did not have to disqualify
herself. I talked with Mrs. Sartor again tonight, and she has indicated
to me that rather than have any possible question, she would just as soon
disqualify herself when the matter comes up on the next agenda which of
course is her prerogative. As to whether or not you own property and you
are within the 300 foot radius, I think it is a fine line question as to
whether or not you have to disqualify yourself. My opinion is if you are
not getting an economic benefit, I am speaking in general terms now, because
different things will come up, there will be times when you ask, and I will
tell you I think you should disqualify yourself; if you are not receiving
a direct or indirect financial benefit from whatever action is going to be
taken, to me I can't see a conflict of interest. Perhaps the better and
more political step to take is to say that I am one of the people who have
been notified and leave it at that. It's one of those things you are just
going to have to question on each individual basis. Mr. Caldwell, again I
will go on record as saying I do not think that you, living on Burning
Tree and Lakewood, have sufficient interest to disqualify yourself.

We had a matter on the Planning Commission agenda last week that was within
300 feet of my house, and I made my statement and just said I would step
down. If they had any legal questions, there is no question legally I
could answer, but that way it keeps everybody happy.

sections.conclusion: Based on the information on pages two and three of your letter, and the fact that the property in which the Council Person and Planning Director have an interest is already developed to maximum density, we agree with your conclusion that it is not "foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect" on that interest in real property. sections.question_synthetic: Whether a City Council member and Planning Director who hold financial interests in property subject to a rezoning ordinance must disqualify themselves from voting on the matter under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC agrees with the City Attorney's conclusion that disqualification is not required because it is not foreseeable that the rezoning decision will have a material financial effect on the officials' property interests, given that the properties are already developed to maximum density and the rezoning actually reduces permissible density.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: This is an incoming request letter (dated January 25, 1977) followed by an FPPC response letter (dated March 29, 1977). Both documents are included. The response letter is relatively brief informal advice rather than a formal opinion. OCR quality is good overall with minor formatting artifacts. The document clearly shows the Q/A structure through the incoming request and FPPC response.

embedding.summary: The City of Victorville requested FPPC guidance on whether a City Council member and Planning Director with financial interests in properties subject to a rezoning ordinance must disqualify themselves. The FPPC provided informal advice agreeing that disqualification was not necessary because no material financial effect was foreseeable, as the properties were already fully developed and the new zoning actually reduced maximum density.
